1: "Dinah, the daughter Leah had borne to Jacob, went out to visit the women of the land" — Dinah's brief introduction (30:21 was her birth notice, without the naming formula given to her brothers) is now expanded. Going out to visit the women of the land was a socially normal activity in pastoral society, where young women's mobility was not as restricted as in urban settings. The narrator identifies her through her mother Leah rather than her father Jacob — emphasizing the matrilineal dimension before the patrilineal response dominates the narrative.
2: "Shechem son of Hamor the Hivite, the ruler of that area, saw her, he took her and raped her" — the narrative is unambiguous: wayyiqqaḥ ʾōtāh wayyiškaḇ ʾōtāh wayyĕʿannehā (he took her and lay with her and humiliated/violated her). The verb ʿinnāh (to violate/humiliate/afflict) is the standard OT term for rape (cf. 2 Samuel 13:12, 14, 22, 32 — Amnon and Tamar). Shechem is identified as son of Hamor (whose name means "donkey" — possibly a clan or personal name) and as the Hivite ruler of the area — a person of significant political authority whose action has both personal and political dimensions.
3: "His heart was drawn to Dinah... he loved the young woman and spoke tenderly to her" — the narrator presents Shechem's emotional state after the rape: attraction, love, and tender speech. "Spoke tenderly" (waydabbēr ʿal-lēḇ hannaʿărāh, spoke to the heart of the young woman) is the same idiom used for comforting speech in Isaiah 40:2 and Ruth 2:13. The juxtaposition of rape and subsequent "love" is not reconciliation of the act but characterization of Shechem as someone who desired what he violated — a dimension of the ANE male-centered view of sexual possession that the narrative presents without endorsing.
4: "Shechem said to his father Hamor, 'Get me this girl as my wife'" — marriage after rape was a recognized (if morally disturbing) resolution in ANE law. Deuteronomy 22:28-29 legislates that a man who rapes an unbetrothed virgin must pay the bride price and marry her (and cannot divorce her). The Shechemite request follows this legal framework: Shechem wants formal marriage, not merely possession. That the ANE legal remedy existed does not justify the rape, but it contextualizes why Hamor's subsequent negotiation (rather than Jacob's retaliation) is the expected social response.
5: "Jacob heard that his daughter Dinah had been defiled (ṭimmēʾ), his sons were in the fields with his livestock; so he did nothing about it until they came home" — Jacob's waiting for his sons is practical — decisions about marriage and family honor required the household's male leadership — but his silence throughout the negotiations that follow is also noteworthy. The defilement vocabulary (ṭimmēʾ, to make ritually/socially impure) frames the assault as a violation not just of Dinah personally but of the household's covenant purity — the same word used for sexual violations in Lev 18 and Num 5.
6: "Shechem's father Hamor went out to talk with Jacob" — the formal negotiation is conducted between the household heads. Hamor, as the city ruler and Shechem's father, has the authority to negotiate on behalf of the Shechemite community. Jacob, as the patriarch of his household, is the corresponding party. The negotiation follows standard ANE marriage-arrangement protocol: the fathers negotiate while the sons are involved as direct parties. The departure from protocol is that this negotiation is about repairing a violation, not initiating a legitimate courtship.
7: "They were shocked and furious, because Shechem had done an outrageous thing in Israel by sleeping with Jacob's daughter — a thing that should not be done" — the phrase nĕḇālāh ʿāśāh bĕyiśrāʾēl (he committed a folly/outrage in Israel) is a technical legal formula that recurs in the OT for gross violations of covenant community standards. In 2 Samuel 13:12, Tamar uses virtually identical language to Amnon before he rapes her: "such a thing should not be done in Israel." In Judg 19-20 (the Gibeah incident), the same formula frames the community's response. The expression functions as a legal finding: this act violates the basic standards of the covenant community.
8: "My son Shechem has his heart set on your daughter. Please give her to him as his wife" — Hamor's appeal to Jacob is framed entirely in terms of Shechem's desire, not Dinah's welfare or Jacob's injury. The ANE marriage negotiation was primarily between the households' male heads; Dinah's own perspective is not sought or mentioned. Hamor's proposal — marriage — is the legally recognized resolution, but the narrative frames it as insufficient given the violation that preceded it.
9: "Intermarry with us; give us your daughters and take our daughters for yourselves" — Hamor's broader proposal goes beyond the single case: full intermarriage between Jacob's household and the Shechemite community. In ANE terms, this was a proposal for covenant alliance — the sharing of daughters in marriage created kinship bonds that were the foundation of political alliances and social integration. The proposal would effectively dissolve the distinction between Jacob's covenant community and the Canaanite city of Shechem — exactly the kind of cultural absorption that the covenant endogamy principle (Genesis 24:3; Genesis 26:34-35; Genesis 28:1) was designed to prevent.
10: "You can settle among us; the land is open to you. Live in it, trade in it, and acquire property in it" — Hamor offers full civic integration: residence rights, trade access, and property acquisition. These three benefits — settling, trading, owning property — were the basic components of civic belonging in ANE city-state culture. Full access to them was restricted to citizens; outsiders (like Jacob's household, camping outside the city) had limited rights. Hamor is offering full citizenship in exchange for the marriage alliance — a substantial economic and legal incentive.
11: "Let me find favor in your eyes, and I will give you whatever you ask" — Shechem's direct appeal to Jacob and the brothers uses the court language of favor-seeking (timṣĕʾû-ḥēn bĕʿênêkem), the same formula Jacob used toward Esau in Genesis 33. Shechem is now in the position of the supplicant, seeking approval from the family whose daughter he violated. The reversal of power — the city ruler now petitioning the semi-nomadic clan — reflects the legal reality that he needs their cooperation for the marriage he wants.
12: "Make the price for the bride and the gift I am to bring as great as you like" — Shechem offers an unlimited mohar (bride price) and mattān (gift). The distinction between the two terms reflects ANE marriage economics: the mohar was the price paid to the bride's family (compensating them for the loss of their daughter's productive labor); the mattān was the gift given directly to the bride herself. Shechem is essentially offering to pay whatever is needed to formalize the marriage — an acknowledgment that the violation has created a debt that must be compensated.
13: "Because their sister Dinah had been defiled, Jacob's sons replied deceitfully" — the narrator's explicit editorial judgment — wayyanû bĕmirmāh (they answered with deceit/treachery) — frames what follows as a morally labeled deception, not a legitimate negotiation. The word mirmāh is the same word used for Jacob's deception of Isaac in Genesis 27 (Genesis 27:35: "your brother came with deceit"). The pattern of generational deception — the sons use the same instrument their father used — is not presented approvingly.
14: "We can't give our sister to a man who is not circumcised. That would be a disgrace to us" — circumcision is the Abrahamic covenant marker (Genesis 17:10-14), the physical sign that distinguished Abraham's household from the surrounding peoples. Using it as the condition for marriage negotiation weaponizes a sacred covenant marker. The "disgrace" (ḥerpāh, the same word Rachel used for the disgrace of barrenness in Genesis 30:23) frames the uncircumcised status of the Shechemites as a social and covenant incompatibility. The demand is not illegitimate in itself — it would produce genuine covenant integration — but the brothers intend something else entirely.
15: "We will enter into an agreement with you on one condition only: that you become like us by circumcising all your males" — the brothers propose a condition that appears to offer genuine integration. If all Shechemite males were circumcised, they would bear the covenant sign of Abraham's household. The proposal is not a complete fraud in its stated terms — it describes a real path to covenant incorporation. The fraud is in the brothers' intention: the condition is set knowing they plan violence, not integration.
16: "Then we will give you our daughters and take your daughters for ourselves. We'll settle among you and become one people with you" — the full intermarriage proposal echoes Hamor's offer in Genesis 34:9. If taken at face value, it would dissolve the boundaries between Jacob's covenant household and Shechem's Canaanite city — the very outcome that covenant endogamy prevents. The brothers are describing an outcome they do not intend, making the whole negotiation a theatrical performance concealing a military plan.
17: "But if you will not agree to be circumcised, we'll take our sister and go" — the ultimatum gives the Shechemites a binary choice: circumcision or Dinah's return and Jacob's household's departure. The threat of departure is credible because Jacob's household has no legal obligation to remain near Shechem. The return of Dinah without marriage settlement was the alternative — but Dinah is currently in Shechem's house (Genesis 34:26 notes that the brothers "took Dinah from Shechem's house"), suggesting she is being held or has been living there since the incident.
18: "Their proposal seemed good to Hamor and his son Shechem" — the Shechemites are persuaded. The proposal is presented as acceptable because (from their perspective) the economic and social benefits (full integration, property rights, intermarriage) outweigh the physical cost of circumcision. The reader, knowing the brothers' real intention, experiences the Shechemites' acceptance as the springing of a trap.
19: "The young man, who was the most honored of all his father's household, lost no time in doing what they said, because he was delighted with Jacob's daughter" — Shechem's social status ("most honored," nikhbāḏ) makes his willingness to be circumcised more significant: he is not compelling others to do what he is unwilling to do. His personal motivation (delight in Dinah) is offered as the explanation for his speed. The narrative continues to present Shechem's desire for Dinah as genuine — making the subsequent slaughter more morally complex rather than a simple story of justified revenge.
20: "So Hamor and his son Shechem went to the gate of their city to speak to the men of their city" — the city gate was the ANE's primary civic assembly space: legal proceedings, commercial transactions, and community decisions were conducted there. Hamor and Shechem's address to the community at the gate is a formal political process — they are not privately enrolling individuals but making a public proposal to the citizen body. The gate as the site of civic decision-making appears throughout the OT (Ruth 4:1-11; Proverbs 31:23) and in ANE texts.
21: "These men are friendly toward us" — Hamor's presentation to the city emphasizes the economic benefits (friendly trade partners, plenty of land) without foregrounding the full context. The word šĕlēmîm (peaceful, friendly) is the masculine plural of the word for shalom — Hamor is vouching for Jacob's household as harmless, a characterization that will prove catastrophically wrong. The presentation omits Shechem's violation of Dinah — the precipitating cause of the negotiation is not disclosed to the citizens who will pay the price.
22: "The men will agree to live with us as one people only on the condition that our males be circumcised, as they themselves are" — Hamor faithfully reports the condition, though Genesis 34:23's economic argument suggests his motivation in presenting it. The two-sided nature of the proposal — they circumcise, Jacob's household integrates — is what makes it persuasive to the community. From the Shechemites' perspective, it is a negotiated settlement between communities, not a trap.
23: "Won't their livestock, their property and all their other animals become ours?" — Hamor's economic argument is the selling point: Jacob's household wealth (substantial from the Laban years) will become communally accessible once they are fully integrated. The argument is that circumcision is a temporary physical cost that will yield permanent economic benefit. The irony is precise: in Genesis 34:28, the brothers seize exactly those livestock and animals as plunder. Hamor's economic promise to his citizens becomes the description of what they lose.
24: "All the men who went out of the city gate agreed with Hamor and his son Shechem, and every male in the city was circumcised" — the unanimous circumcision of the entire male population follows the civic decision made at the gate. The phrase "all the men who went out of the city gate" (kol-yōṣĕʾê šaʿar ʿîrô) — the city's full male citizen body — indicates complete community participation. The circumcision is not a token compliance but a total commitment. The covenant sign of Abraham's people has now been applied to an entire Canaanite population — under false pretenses, by both sides of the negotiation.
25: "Three days later, while all of them were still in pain, two of Jacob's sons, Simeon and Levi, Dinah's brothers, took their swords and attacked the unsuspecting city, killing every male" — the third day after circumcision is the most physically debilitating. ANE medical knowledge recognized this; the timing of the attack on the third day was deliberate military intelligence applied to a specific vulnerability. "Unsuspecting" (beṭaḥ, in security/safety) — the city was not on a military footing; they had just concluded what they believed was a peaceful integration agreement. Simeon and Levi are identified specifically as "Dinah's brothers" — children of Leah, full brothers to Dinah, with a specific honor-obligation to defend their full sister.
26: "They put Hamor and his son Shechem to the sword and took Dinah from Shechem's house and left" — the targeting of Hamor and Shechem specifically (the ruler and his son who violated and detained Dinah) is distinguished from the general massacre. Dinah's removal "from Shechem's house" confirms she has been held there — whether voluntarily or as a de facto hostage during the negotiations. The phrase raises the question of Dinah's own experience and voice, which the narrative does not give her.
27: "The sons of Jacob came upon the dead and looted the city where their sister had been defiled" — after Simeon and Levi's military action, the rest of Jacob's sons participate in plundering the city. The looting follows the ANE practice of taking enemy property after military victory; it extends the punishment from the specific perpetrators (Hamor and Shechem) to the entire community. The narrator's framing — "where their sister had been defiled" — maintains the justificatory connection between the plunder and the original wrong.
28: "They seized their flocks and herds and donkeys and everything else of theirs in the city and out in the fields" — the specific list of plunder — flocks, herds, donkeys, city property, field property — is exactly the wealth catalog that Hamor promised his citizens would become theirs through integration (Genesis 34:23). The economic logic is inverted: instead of Jacob's household's wealth flowing into Shechem, Shechem's wealth flows out to Jacob's household. The irony underscores the catastrophic miscalculation of Hamor's economic argument.
29: "They carried off all their wealth and all their women and children, taking as plunder everything in the houses" — the capture of women and children as slaves was standard ANE warfare practice; it does not mean the narrator endorses it. The totality of the plunder — wealth, women, children, household goods — leaves the city of Shechem stripped. What began as a negotiation for one woman's marriage ends with the forced displacement of an entire city's women and children.
30: "You have brought trouble on me by making me obnoxious to the Canaanites and Perizzites" — Jacob's rebuke to Simeon and Levi is entirely pragmatic: his concern is his own safety and reputation (ʿăkhartem ʾōtî, you have made me a stink/trouble), not the ethics of the massacre or the wellbeing of the Shechemite women and children. "We are few in number" — Jacob's household is demographically vulnerable; a coalition of Canaanite city-states in retaliation could destroy them. The rebuke reveals Jacob's self-protective orientation; he has no moral argument to offer, only a survival argument.
31: "Should he have treated our sister like a prostitute?" — Simeon and Levi's rhetorical counter challenges Jacob's silence on the moral substance. The word "prostitute" (zônāh) captures what Shechem's violation without immediate marriage would have meant socially: treating Dinah as sexually available without covenant status — a woman without honor-protection. Their counter argues that Jacob's pragmatic concern for his own safety failed to address the fundamental covenant wrong. The narrative ends without resolution — both positions are stated; neither is adjudicated by the narrator, leaving the reader to weigh the competing claims. Genesis 49:5-7 revisits this, where Jacob curses Simeon and Levi for the violence at Shechem.
